behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler+signature verifier+queue handoff. A pure unit test of HMAC logic can miss wiring bugs (e.g., parsed body vs raw body, or enqueue still called on 401). No real OS/network/subprocess is required, and no AI/ML dependency exists, so System/Agentic are not required.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler in-process with a real raw-body capture path and a spy/fake queue module. Send a request with mismatched `X-Signature-256` and assert `401` plus zero enqueue calls.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is ordering across modules (HTTP handler and persistent queue). Unit is too low because ordering bugs appear at module handoff. Higher levels were considered but rejected because behavior text only requires local persistence ordering, not real network/subprocess: "written to the persistent queue before ... 200".
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process HTTP invocation wired to the real queue adapter against a local test persistence backend (e.g., temp SQLite/file). Delay queue write completion and assert response is not sent until write commit succeeds.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure is async boundary between enqueue/ack and downstream worker execution. Integration is enough to catch accidental `await` on processing path. System was considered but rejected because no real external infra is required by behavior text; in-process delayed worker reproduces the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream processor in one process. Make processor block (promise resolves later), send valid event, assert HTTP response returns before processor completion while event remains queued/processing continues asynchronously.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time concurrency/SLO behavior under load; scheduler, event loop contention, and real HTTP serving characteristics can hide failures in in-process mocks. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE push to System. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start the actual service process, issue concurrent HTTP webhook requests while earlier events trigger intentionally slow downstream processing, and measure end-to-end wall-clock latency of new requests; assert responses stay under 3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at handler+idempotency store/queue interaction boundary. Unit tests of a dedup helper miss race/wiring issues. System was considered but rejected because behavior text does not require real network/subprocess; local persistence-backed integration test is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Use handler with real dedup/persistence wiring in test storage. Send same event ID twice and assert only one queue record/write exists and second request is handled without second enqueue.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is mapping dependency state (`queue full`) to HTTP contract (status/header) across module boundary. Unit could miss translation/wiring errors. System was considered but rejected because the behavior is explicitly conditional on queue report and can be reproduced with in-process queue implementation.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue module configured to return a full-capacity error. Send valid request and assert `503` plus `Retry-After` header, and no downstream processing start.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This validates queue component + persistence behavior with real local storage semantics; unit fakes can hide enforcement bugs. System was considered but rejected because no external network/subprocess is required by behavior text; local DB/filesystem-backed queue is enough.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue against a local test backend with capacity `N`, enqueue `N` events, then enqueue one more and assert explicit capacity rejection and no over-capacity persisted record.
